nativeRange.habitat: Shallow inshore and back-reef zones, patch reefs, and shallow tidal lagoons with strong currents; found on hard substrate from the surface to moderate depths

decorPreferences: secure base epoxied or glued to live rock
placed upright with branches oriented perpendicular to dominant flow
adequate clearance on all sides to prevent tissue contact with neighboring corals
middle to upper placement for moderate-high light access
stable rockwork anchor point to prevent toppling

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae. Polyps also filter fine zooplankton and phytoplankton from the water column. Supplemental target-feeding of fine planktonic foods (rotifers, copepod nauplii, marine snow, oyster eggs) weekly has been reported to support colony health and growth. Photosynthesis typically provides the bulk of nutritional needs under adequate lighting.

goodWith: other photosynthetic gorgonians with adequate spacing
non-aggressive LPS and soft corals with spacing
avoidWith: flamingo tongue snails (Cyphoma gibbosum — obligate gorgonian predators)
large angelfish and butterflyfish (potential polyp nippers)
corals placed in direct tissue contact (risk of stinging and algae fouling along contact points)
aggressive or rapidly growing encrusting corals nearby

imageCandidates.notes: Roger A. Morales-Flores; ~1153x2048. P. anceps (Purple Blade Gorgonian): vibrant purple-violet flat blades, whole colony on rock in bright sunlit shallow water. Striking, sharp, diagnostic color - the hobby's 'purple blade' look.
Matteo Cassella; ~1796x2048. P. anceps whole colony (flat blades) in situ with a hawkfish sheltering among branches; brown-purple daylight coloration.
Robin White; 2048x1536. P. anceps close-up under blue reef light: purple-edged blades with white feeding polyps extended, sharp detail. Feature shot (not whole colony).
Robin White; ~1536x2048. P. anceps blade branches with white polyps fully extended, in situ; sharp close-up showing polyp detail.
Robin White; 2048x1536. P. citrina (Yellow Sea Whip / Green Lace): vibrant yellow-green bushy colony with white polyps extended. Sharp, diagnostic yellow color.
Matteo Bellucci; 2048x1536. P. citrina whole colony, tan-yellow branches densely covered with extended white polyps; sharp in-situ shot.
Josiah Londeree; ~2048x1536. P. citrina green 'lace' form - two feathery green colonies on sand, showing the bushy branching. Green-cast daylight; adds a color variant.
Robin White; 2048x1536. P. citrina whole yellow colony in reef context with polyps extended. Minor 'Robin White' watermark bottom-right.
Robin White; ~1155x2048. P. guadalupensis (Grooved-Blade Sea Whip): tall cream grooved blades with lilac-white polyps fully extended; sharp, whole colony, colorful reef backdrop.
Robin White; ~1155x2048. P. guadalupensis - three upright grooved whips with dense white polyps extended; sharp in-situ detail of the whip-like form.
Robert Webster; ~1024x767. P. guadalupensis whole colony over sand, showing the angular blade-whip branching; bluish daylight color, sharp.

summary: Pterogorgia Corals are a genus of photosynthetic Caribbean gorgonians with distinctive flat-bladed or angular branching forms. They are generally considered intermediate-level corals, with high, non-laminar water movement widely regarded as the single most critical husbandry factor.
careNotes:
This entry covers the three species of zooxanthellate (photosynthetic) gorgonian corals in the genus Pterogorgia, all endemic to the Western Atlantic and Caribbean: P. anceps (Angular Sea Whip or Purple Blade Gorgonian), P. citrina (Yellow Sea Whip or Green Lace Gorgonian), and P. guadalupensis (Grooved-Blade Sea Whip). All three share near-identical husbandry requirements and are generally discussed at genus level in the hobby.

In the wild, Pterogorgia species have been documented in very shallow tidal lagoons, back reefs, and patch reef environments subject to strong wave action and tidal currents — shallower and more turbulent habitat than many other Caribbean gorgonians.

**Flow (critical — the most important parameter):** High, non-laminar water movement is widely reported as the single most important captive husbandry factor. One keeper account describes difficulty with this genus until flow was substantially increased, after which polyp extension became consistent. Wave-type or alternating flow (gyre pumps, wave makers) is preferred over direct laminar blasting. The flat-bladed branches should be oriented perpendicular to the dominant current so flow passes across the blade faces.

**Lighting:** Moderate to high intensity (approximately 100–250 PAR) is generally advised. Pterogorgia is photosynthetic and has been documented in very shallow, sunlit Caribbean waters, where adequate light is important for colony health. Gradual acclimation from lower PAR positions is recommended for newly acquired specimens.

**Placement:** Generally advised in the middle portion of the aquarium with clear water movement around all branches. Placing branches too close to rock surfaces or neighboring corals increases the risk of algae fouling along contact points.

**Algae fouling:** Insufficient flow or elevated nutrients can allow algae to colonize the branches and skeleton. Keeping nitrate below 20 ppm, phosphate below 0.1 ppm, and ensuring strong unobstructed flow around the entire colony are the primary prevention measures.

**Note on non-photosynthetic confusion:** Some trade sources and databases incorrectly list Pterogorgia as non-photosynthetic. All three species in this genus are confirmed zooxanthellate, meaning they harbor symbiotic zooxanthellae and obtain much of their nutrition through photosynthesis. Any care guidance recommending heavy daily feeding is most likely written for azooxanthellate (non-photosynthetic) gorgonians and does not apply to Pterogorgia.

**Species variants sold in trade:**
- P. anceps has flat, angular, blade-like purple-violet branches and is most commonly sold as 'Purple Blade Gorgonian' or 'Purple Ribbon Gorgonian'; colonies can reach 60–100 cm.
- P. citrina has yellow-green, bushy branching and is sold as 'Yellow Sea Whip', 'Green Lace Gorgonian', or 'Gold Ribbon Gorgonian'; colonies are typically smaller, ranging from 25–46 cm.
- P. guadalupensis is less commonly available in the trade.
breedingNotes: In the wild, Pterogorgia reproduces sexually through broadcast spawning, in which eggs and sperm are released into the water column. In captivity, fragmentation has been reported as possible — the flat blade branches can be cut and mounted on rubble or frag plugs — though gorgonians are generally regarded as more delicate to propagate this way than stony corals and require careful handling. Captive sexual reproduction is not documented in the hobby.

primary.name: Reeflings – Angular Sea Whip (Pterogorgia anceps) Complete Care Guide
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters (temp 24–28°C, SG 1.023–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, PAR 150–300), depth range up to 30m, flow requirements, placement (middle to top), care level (moderate to easy), temperament (peaceful), photosynthetic status confirmed.

additional.name: TankFacts – Green Lace Gorgonian (Pterogorgia citrina)
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temp 22–26°C, SG 1.023–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, kH 8–12, Ca 380–450 ppm, Mg 1200–1400 ppm. Confirmed peaceful temperament, moderate-strong flow, photosynthetic with supplemental feeding. Minor disagreement on temperature upper bound (26°C vs 28°C from Reeflings); averaged to 27°C max.

additional.name: Reeflex – Pterogorgia citrina Yellow Sea Whip
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (Brazil, Florida, Gulf of Mexico, Bahamas, Caribbean, West Atlantic), depth 1–27m, colony size 25–46 cm, minimum tank 100L. Note: source incorrectly classifies citrina as azooxanthellate; this was rejected based on all other sources confirming photosynthetic status. Daily feeding recommendation discarded as non-applicable to photosynthetic specimens.

additional.name: Reeflex NO – Pterogorgia anceps (Norwegian)
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (Belize, Florida, Bahamas, Caribbean), depth 1–20m, colony size 60–100 cm. Minimum tank listed as 800L appeared to assume non-photosynthetic husbandry; discarded in favor of lower but realistic estimate.

additional.name: Reef Builders – Photosynthetic Gorgonians for the Home Aquaria
additional.notes: Confirmed Pterogorgia occurs in shallow tidal lagoons; strong lighting and high flow identified as critical for this genus specifically. Keeper account confirming polyp retraction resolved by increasing flow.
